meta.title: Managed Household Staff — Playable Case Study
meta.hubTagline: The branded, trained, background-verified and actively-managed household-staff service Mumbai’s affluent homes can finally trust — with a replacement guarantee no informal hire offers.

hero.sub: A playable walk-through of the Managed Household Staff dossier. Same facts, same numbers — but you get to investigate, decide, and vote.

opening.lead: India’s affluent run their homes on a workforce sourced through WhatsApp groups and watchmen, vetted by nobody, accountable to no one — and they know it. No organised player owns the premium, actively-managed end. That gap is the whole thesis.

stats.labelHtml: of India’s UHNWIs (US$30M+) live in Mumbai — the single largest concentration (Knight Frank)
venture funding into quick, gig, cheap house-help (Snabbit, Pronto) — while the premium managed niche sits ignored
of families do any proper due-diligence before letting a stranger live in their home (OnGrid)
funded operators bundling premium + trained + actively-managed + guaranteed for UHNI households — the whitespace, quantified

imgband.title: Three unsatisfying choices
imgband.sub: Downmarket placement marketplaces with a 1/5 reputation · premium agencies that stop at recruitment and vanish · 10-minute gig apps built for cheap and fast. The managed, accountable middle is empty.

whitespace.lead: Eight real players, plotted. Hover any dot. Notice where nobody is: premium-priced and actively-managed with a guarantee. That corner is what this business is built to own.

players.d: premium + training, but stops at recruitment
luxury concierge — errands, not resident staff

whitespace.sidebarHtml:
Reading the map.

The scaled leader BookMyBai has a guarantee but is downmarket and reputationally damaged. EliteButlers is the only true premium incumbent — but publishes no managed retainer or replacement SLA. The gig wave (Snabbit, Pronto, Urban Company) is cheap, fast and high-frequency — the opposite end.

The top-right — premium and actively-managed — is uncontested. That is the investable idea.

hook.lead: Every affluent Mumbai family believes it has a safe way to staff its home. Real diligence tested each one against primary sources. Click each case file to see the verdict — and watch every safety net fail.
hook.statusInitialTail: open them all to close the case.
hook.statusSolvedHtml: Case closed. Every existing ‘trust signal’ fails — the referral, the police form, the scaled leader, the premium incumbent, the gig app. The only thing that survives scrutiny is a trained, verified, actively-managed layer with a real replacement guarantee — which no one is selling. That absence is the opportunity.

cases.name: The watchman referral
cases.det: Fewer than 10% of families do any proper due-diligence (OnGrid) — most rely on a photocopied ID and a verbal reference. Trust here is a feeling, not a check.

cases.name: Mumbai Police verification
cases.det: Mumbai Police runs a dedicated online domestic-servant verification form precisely because theft and misconduct are live concerns — yet almost no one uses it. The safeguard exists on paper only.

cases.name: BookMyBai
cases.det: Carries a 1/5 rating on MouthShut — 2025 complaints of fake profiles, staff who ‘can’t cook,’ and replacements that never arrived. Trusted-but-poor-quality: it has scale and a guarantee, but no management layer.

cases.name: EliteButlers
cases.det: Genuine premium positioning and training — but publishes no ongoing employer-of-record, payroll-management or replacement SLA. It stops at recruitment, then the family is on its own again.

cases.name: Urban Company Insta Help
cases.det: Gig hourly (₹49–245/hr), not full-time, not premium — and it drew a public labour-ethics backlash. It sets the low-price anchor, not a trusted managed alternative.

cases.name: The managed EoR retainer
cases.det: Recruit → vet → train → place → keep managing: payroll, supervision, QC, compliance and a replacement SLA. No single player bundles this at scale for UHNI homes — the one option that survives scrutiny doesn’t exist yet.

personas.lead: Every persona has a trigger, an objection, and one thing that converts them. Flip each card to see what wins the sale.

items.name: The Malabar Hill Family
items.trig: A multi-property home running 5–10 staff with no chief-of-staff layer; coordination chaos; a single bad hire is a genuine security risk.
items.obj: Discretion and security are paramount — will an outsider really be safer than the people they already know?
items.conv: An estate-manager tier that runs the household so they don’t have to, plus employer-of-record accountability — they pay for accountability, not price. Reached via private banks and luxury real estate.

items.name: The Rao Family
items.trig: Back from London/Dubai/Singapore, already used to agencies, contracts and payroll — and frustrated by India’s cash-based informality.
items.obj: “Does a properly managed, contracted service even exist here?” — scepticism that India can match what they left behind.
items.conv: A familiar, contracted, compliant, done-properly model — the closest thing to home. They arrive already primed to pay for management, via NRI and relocation networks.

items.name: The Powai Couple
items.trig: Both work long hours in finance/tech; no time or expertise to recruit, train, supervise or run payroll and compliance.
items.obj: “Is a retainer worth it when I could just hire a bai for ₹18k?” — budget-anchored to the informal norm.
items.conv: ‘Done-for-me’ delegation: start with one or two roles, expand once trust is built. Sold through employer and premium-building channels.

items.name: The Founder Household
items.trig: Sudden liquidity (IPO/exit); scaling a home and lifestyle fast with no legacy staff network.
items.obj: Doesn’t know how to hire well and wants to avoid rookie mistakes — but is wary of paying a premium for a ‘nicer website’.
items.conv: A turnkey, get-it-right-first-time branded solution rather than a WhatsApp gamble — highest willingness in the setup window, converting to a standing retainer via the Aamara ecosystem.

tycoon.lead: Four real decisions from the dossier. Each has a right answer the diligence already argues for. Pick — then see what the evidence says.

decisions.ctx: The defining fork. It changes which laws bind you, the GST base, and the entire cost structure.

opts.t: Pure marketplace — broker and disappear, keep it light
opts.fb: Brokering via an app likely makes you an ‘aggregator’ with a Social Security Fund levy and workers as gig, not employees — and, fatally, no control to make the trust, guarantee and QC real. You’d be rebuilding BookMyBai.

opts.t: Employer-of-record / managed (hybrid at the low tier)
opts.fb: Correct. Heavier compliance — EPF/ESIC/gratuity, GST-on-gross — but that compliance is the moat and the ESG story, and it’s what makes the trust, control and replacement guarantee real. Price the retainer to absorb it.

decisions.ctx: Over ₹1,200 cr has poured into cheap, fast, gig house-help. Pick your lane.

opts.t: Undercut the gig apps on price and frequency
opts.fb: That’s Snabbit/Pronto/Urban Company’s DNA — cheap, fast, high-frequency — and they’re better capitalised for that fight. Competing on the salary (which is pass-through) is a race you can’t win and don’t need to.

opts.t: Own the empty premium, actively-managed, guaranteed box
opts.fb: Correct. This is a trust product for the top 1%, not a price product. BookMyBai has scale but is downmarket; EliteButlers is premium but unmanaged — each misses the decisive attribute you can own.

decisions.q: 3 · How do you reach the buyer?
decisions.ctx: Purchase is triggered by a life event — a new home, a return from abroad, a bad hire. Pick the motion.

opts.t: Performance marketing and paid search at scale
opts.fb: A trust product for a tiny, referral-dense UHNI community doesn’t convert on ads. You’d burn CAC reaching the wrong people at the wrong moment.

opts.t: Referral & partner-led — designers, private banks, brokers, Aamara’s network
opts.fb: Correct. Reach the household at the trigger moment through the designers, private banks and brokers already in their lives — and Aamara’s first-party HNI network is the one asset a funded competitor can’t buy.

decisions.q: 4 · How do you stop clients poaching your trained staff?
decisions.ctx: The central sensitivity: a family takes the trained staff and drops the retainer.

opts.t: A tight non-solicit clause and hope it holds
opts.fb: A contract alone won’t hold against informal cash. Lean only on legal deterrence and you’ve left the thesis’s biggest risk unmanaged.

opts.t: Four locks — ongoing retainer value, non-solicit + buy-out, staff loyalty, loss-of-guarantee
opts.fb: Correct. The retainer’s value is ongoing (payroll, compliance, replacement, QC), staff loyalty is to the company (PF, career path, bonuses), and going direct forfeits the guarantee — the exact risk they were paying to avoid. This is what the Mumbai pilot exists to prove.

human.titleHtml: The most important ₹ in the deck.
human.lead: The moat isn’t marketing — it’s a career ladder. Today’s informal live-in maid earns ₹16–20k in cash with no PF, no security, no path. Formal, certified employment (maid → cook → estate manager) can reach ₹1 lakh+ — worth more inside the system than outside it. That ladder is both the ethics and the anti-poaching lock.

rows.label: Estate / house manager — top of ladder

human.note: The workforce is overwhelmingly female, migrant, Dalit, Adivasi and Muslim, and 90%+ informal. Registered employment — PF, benefits, academy certification, a real path — beats informal cash on the one thing a good cook or estate manager most lacks: stability. That switching cost is what keeps trained staff exclusive to you, and it’s a genuine family-office ESG story rather than a bolt-on one.

quiz.lead: Five questions. Then you cast the vote you’d cast in the room.

items.q: What is the empty box the whole thesis rests on?
opts: Cheaper maids than the gig apps
Premium + trained + actively-managed (employer-of-record) + guaranteed replacement, at scale for UHNI households
A domestic-worker app nobody has built
A concierge membership for errands
items.e: No single player bundles all four attributes. BookMyBai has scale and a guarantee but is downmarket and unmanaged; EliteButlers is premium and trains staff but publishes no managed retainer or replacement SLA — each misses the decisive attribute.

items.q: Why isn’t BookMyBai the winner despite scale and a guarantee?
opts: It’s too expensive
It only serves Delhi
It’s downmarket, unmanaged and reputationally damaged — 1/5 on MouthShut, with fake profiles and un-honoured replacements
It refuses to offer a guarantee
items.e: The category’s scaled leader is trusted-but-poor-quality: a 1/5 rating, 2025 complaints of fake profiles and replacements that never arrived. That reputation gap is exactly why the premium end sits wide open.

items.q: What is the single biggest risk the dossier names?
opts: A competitor with a nicer website
The informal-market undercut and direct-poaching loop — a family takes the trained staff and drops the retainer
Lack of wealthy households in Mumbai
The coming domestic-worker law banning the model
items.e: Because retainer revenue is per-household not per-person, the model survives staff churn — but only if the anti-poaching loop holds. That loop is the central sensitivity the Mumbai pilot exists to prove before any expansion capital.

items.q: Which operating lane does the dossier recommend, and why?
opts: Pure broker — lightest compliance
Employer-of-record / managed (hybrid at the low tier) — heavier compliance, but that compliance IS the moat
Franchise the brand nationally on day one
Gig-aggregator under e-Shram only
items.e: Employer-of-record adds EPF/ESIC/gratuity and GST-on-gross, but it’s what makes the trust, control and guarantee real — and it’s precisely what a family cannot replicate through informal hiring. The retainer is priced to absorb it.

items.q: Why should Aamara — a family office — run this rather than buy a proven business?
opts: Domestic staffing has guaranteed returns
Because the premium managed version doesn’t exist yet — the absence IS the opportunity, and Aamara’s first-party UHNI network is distribution a startup can’t buy
It’s a tax shelter
Domestic labour is free
items.e: The proven version isn’t for sale because no one has built it. Reputation in a tiny, referral-dense UHNI community plus family-office pedigree compound over time — a relationship asset a funded competitor cannot replicate.

vote.prompt: You’ve seen the whitespace, the trust illusion, the personas, the unit economics and the human number. What’s your call?
vote.yesLabel: Back it — proceed to the Mumbai pilot
vote.yesMsg: Backed. Your first move (per the Action Plan, Days 1–30): lock the employ-vs-broker legal structure with counsel, incorporate and register (GST + Maharashtra Shops & Establishments), sign a BGV vendor (AuthBridge/IDfy/OnGrid) with DPDP-compliant flows, and warm-map 30–50 target UHNI households via Aamara’s network plus 3–4 designer/private-bank referral partners. Patient, margin-and-moat capital — not blitz-scale.
vote.noMsg: Passed — and defensibly so. The dossier agrees the variance is real: a self-constructed, bottom-up TAM with no clean market report; a high-touch model that resists scaling; unproven willingness to pay a recurring retainer above the informal norm; and the informal-undercut-and-poaching loop that could quietly gut the annuity. A pass here is a legitimate IC position — the case study never pretends otherwise.

footer.note1: This playable walk-through is a companion to the full institutional dossier — it simplifies for engagement and never overrides the source document.
footer.note2: Every number here is drawn verbatim from the dossier, including its EST. flags and unverified-source caveats.
